agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, aligned along domain seams. The decomposition prioritizes deployment independence (the stated goal), respects the high-throughput location tracking constraint (5K writes/sec), isolates payment consistency requirements, and stays within the operational capacity of an 8-engineer team. A Strangler Fig migration strategy is recommended — extract high-value services first (Location Tracking, then Payments), leaving lower-risk domains for later phases.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment workflow by emitting domain events.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/collection. Does NOT own courier assignment logic or payment state. Communicates with Payment and Dispatch via async events and synchronous queries where consistency demands it."},{"name":"Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment, route optimization, and dispatch decisions. Matches available couriers to pending orders using location data and courier availability.","boundaries":"Owns courier-to-order assignment state and routing algorithms. Consumes location data from Location Service via internal API. Does not own courier profiles or order details."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS coordinates. Optimized for high write throughput (5K writes/sec peak). Exposes current and historical position queries.","boundaries":"Owns all location/GPS data. Append-only write path. No business logic beyond geo-indexing and TTL-based retention. Isolated data store (time-series or Redis) — not shared with other services."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization, capture, refunds, and ledger reconciliation. Enforces strict transactional consistency on all monetary operations.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, transaction ledger, and integration with payment gateway(s). Synchronous API for payment operations. No eventual consistency — uses ACID transactions internally."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Consumes domain events and applies notification rules/templates.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery preferences, and send logs. Stateless regarding business domain — purely reactive to events. Owns delivery channel integrations (SendGrid, Twilio, APNs/FCM)."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account management: registration, profile, preferences, authentication tokens.","boundaries":"Owns customer profiles and auth data. Exposes a lightweight identity API consumed by all other services for auth/context. Relatively stable — low change frequency."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous REST/gRPC: POST /payments/authorize, POST /payments/capture, POST /payments/refund. Order Service awaits payment confirmation before confirming order.","coupling_type":"synchronous — justified by consistency requirement"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Async event: OrderConfirmed → Dispatch Service subscribes and initiates courier assignment. Dispatch emits CourierAssigned back.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (event-driven)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"Synchronous query: GET /couriers/nearby?lat=X&lng=Y&radius=R. Used during assignment to find closest available couriers.","coupling_type":"synchronous read — low-latency requirement"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Async event: CourierLocationUpdated (high frequency, fan-out limited to Dispatch). Used for live ETA recalculation.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (event stream)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async events: OrderConfirmed, OrderDispatched, OrderDelivered → Notification Service maps to customer-facing messages.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async event: CourierAssigned, CourierNearby → triggers push notifications.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"Synchronous query: GET /customers/{id} for order context. Lightweight, cacheable.","coupling_type":"synchronous read — cacheable"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async event: PaymentFailed, RefundProcessed → triggers payment-related notifications.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"}],"data_flow":"1) Customer places order → Order Service validates, calls Payment Service synchronously to authorize funds. 2) On success, Order Service emits OrderConfirmed event. 3) Dispatch Service consumes event, queries Location Service for nearby couriers, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. 4) Couriers push GPS every 10s → Location Service ingests at high throughput, stores in time-series DB, streams updates to Dispatch for live ETA. 5) Order status transitions (dispatched, delivered) emit events consumed by Notification Service, which fans out to email/SMS/push. 6) On delivery, Order Service calls Payment Service to capture funds. Refunds follow reverse flow.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Team has 3 years of monolith — big-bang rewrite is high risk. Extract one service at a time behind a facade, validate, then cut over. Reduces blast radius."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Async Messaging)","rationale":"Decouples services temporally. Notification, Dispatch, and Order don't need to be available simultaneously. Enables independent deployment — the primary goal."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its data store. Eliminates shared-database coupling which is the #1 monolith trap in decomposition. Location Service gets a time-series store; Payment gets ACID-compliant RDBMS; others use PostgreSQL."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service has asymmetric read/write profiles: 5K writes/sec vs. bursty reads for dispatch queries. Separate write (append-only ingestion) and read (geo-indexed query) paths."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients. Handles auth, rate limiting, and routes to appropriate service. Prevents clients from coupling to internal service topology."},{"pattern":"Saga (Order Fulfillment)","rationale":"Order placement spans Payment and Dispatch. Use a choreography-based saga: Order Service emits events, each service reacts and emits completion/failure. Compensating actions handle failures (e.g., release payment auth if dispatch fails)."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, contains OrderItems, tracks OrderStatus lifecycle)","OrderFulfillment (tracks assignment-to-delivery progress)"]},{"name":"Dispatch & Routing","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: AssignmentId, links OrderId to CourierId, tracks acceptance/rejection)","CourierAvailability (root: CourierId, tracks on-duty status, current capacity)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: CourierId, current lat/lng/timestamp, append-only history)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: TransactionId, links to OrderId, tracks auth/capture/refund lifecycle)","Ledger (append-only record of all monetary movements)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, channel, recipient, template, delivery status)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["CustomerProfile (root: CustomerId, contact info, preferences, auth credentials)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; Dispatch consumes it. Order team accommodates Dispatch needs in event schema."},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Conformist. Dispatch conforms to Location's position data model. Location optimizes for throughput, not Dispatch convenience."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Partnership. Both teams must coordinate on payment lifecycle events. Shared integration tests."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Open Host Service. Order Management publishes well-defined events; Notification Service maps them to templates. No upstream accommodation needed."},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Open Host Service. Same pattern — Dispatch events trigger notifications."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (minimal). CustomerId type shared across contexts. Customer Service is upstream authority on identity."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services for 8 engineers (ratio ~1.3 engineers per service)","gains":"Clean domain boundaries, independent deployment (stated goal), each service is cognitively manageable","sacrifices":"Operational overhead — 8 engineers must operate 6 services + message broker + API gateway. On-call rotation is thin.","alternatives_considered":["Fewer services (3-4): merge Order+Dispatch+Customer into one. Lower ops overhead but reintroduces deployment coupling between order and dispatch — contradicts primary goal.","More services (8+): e.g., separate Courier Profile service. Over-decomposition for team size — would create excessive cross-service coordination overhead."]},{"decision":"Async events as primary integration (except Payment)","gains":"Temporal decoupling, independent deployability, natural resilience to partial outages","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — order status may lag behind reality by seconds. Debugging distributed event flows is harder than tracing monolith calls.","alternatives_considered":["All synchronous: simpler mental model but creates a distributed monolith — defeats the purpose.","All async including payment: violates strict consistency requirement for monetary operations."]},{"decision":"Dedicated time-series store for Location Service","gains":"Handles 5K writes/sec without contending with OLTP workloads. Purpose-built indexing for geo-temporal queries.","sacrifices":"Another database technology to operate and maintain. Team needs to learn time-series DB ops.","alternatives_considered":["Redis with geo commands: simpler ops, but less durable for historical tracking.","PostgreSQL with PostGIS: familiar tech, but write throughput at 5K/sec requires careful tuning and may contend with other workloads."]},{"decision":"Choreography-based saga over orchestration","gains":"No single orchestrator to become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Each service owns its reaction logic.","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize end-to-end flow. Debugging failures requires correlating events across services.","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga (central coordinator): easier to reason about but creates coupling — the orchestrator must know about all participants."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, in-app) are additive — add a new adapter in Notification Service without touching any other service."},{"area":"Routing algorithms","evolution_path":"Dispatch Service encapsulates all routing logic. Can swap from greedy nearest-courier to ML-optimized routing without affecting Order or Location services."},{"area":"Payment providers","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or adding a second provider) is isolated to Payment Service."},{"area":"Location storage technology","evolution_path":"Location Service owns its data store. Can migrate from TimescaleDB to InfluxDB or Redis without ripple effects, since consumers use the service API, not the DB."},{"area":"Event broker","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (SQS/SNS, Cloud Pub/Sub, or managed Kafka). Can upgrade to dedicated Kafka cluster if throughput demands grow, since services interact via topic abstractions."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order-to-Payment synchronous dependency","reason":"Order confirmation requires payment authorization. This coupling is inherent to the domain — you cannot confirm an order without authorized payment. Designed to be rigid because correctness trumps flexibility here."},{"area":"Event schema contracts","reason":"Once services depend on event schemas (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned), changing schemas requires coordinated versioning. Use schema registry and backward-compatible evolution from day one."},{"area":"CustomerId as cross-context identifier","reason":"All services reference CustomerId. Changing the identity model (e.g., merging accounts) has system-wide impact. Invest in getting this right early."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational overload: 8 engineers operating 6 services + infrastructure (message broker, API gateway, multiple databases)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Phase the migration over 6-9 months. Start with Location Service extraction (highest write pressure, most isolated). Invest in shared observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging) before extracting the second service. Use managed services aggressively (managed DB, managed message broker)."},{"risk":"Distributed debugging complexity — event-driven flows are hard to trace","severity":"high","mitigation":"Mandate correlation IDs on every event and API call from day one. Implement distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) as a prerequisite before the first extraction. Build a simple saga status dashboard."},{"risk":"Data consistency gaps during migration — dual writes between monolith and new services","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler Fig with event-based sync. During migration, monolith publishes events that new service consumes (or vice versa). Run shadow-mode validation before cutting over."},{"risk":"Location Service throughput — 5K writes/sec is manageable today but growth could push to 50K+","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Design Location Service write path as stateless and horizontally scalable from day one. Use a partitioned time-series store. Add a write buffer (e.g., Kafka topic) in front of the DB to absorb bursts."},{"risk":"Team knowledge silos — each engineer becomes sole expert on 'their' service","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Rotate service ownership quarterly. Require cross-service code reviews. Document service contracts and runbooks as first-class artifacts."},{"risk":"Saga failure handling — compensating actions for payment+dispatch failures are complex","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Enumerate all failure scenarios explicitly before implementing. Build a dead-letter queue for failed events with manual replay tooling. Start with simple timeout-based compensation."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Strangler Fig migration in 3 phases over 6-9 months. PHASE 1 (Months 1-3): Extract Location Service. It has the most distinct technical requirements (time-series writes at 5K/sec), minimal business logic coupling, and clear API boundaries. Stand up the event broker and observability stack during this phase. PHASE 2 (Months 3-6): Extract Payment Service. Strict consistency requirements make it critical to isolate early. This also decouples the most sensitive regulatory surface area. PHASE 3 (Months 6-9): Extract Dispatch, Notification, and Customer services. By now the team has operational muscle memory. Dispatch is the most complex extraction (depends on Location reads + Order events) so save it for when the team is experienced. Keep Order Management in the monolith longest — it touches the most other domains and benefits from lessons learned.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Decision record for the 6-service split","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — Why async events over synchronous calls","docs/adr/003-location-service-data-store.md — Time-series DB selection rationale","docs/service-contracts/location-service-api.yaml — OpenAPI spec for Location Service","docs/service-contracts/events-schema.json — JSON Schema registry for domain events (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned, etc.)","docs/runbooks/saga-failure-handling.md — Failure scenarios and compensating actions","infra/docker-compose.local.yml — Local dev environment with all services + broker","services/location-service/ — First extracted service (Phase 1)","services/payment-service/ — Second extraction (Phase 2)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Database-per-service with no shared databases. Correlation IDs on all events and API calls. Schema versioning with backward compatibility. Circuit breakers on all synchronous cross-service calls. Health checks and readiness probes on every service. Structured logging with service name, correlation ID, and timestamp.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Shared database between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Synchronous call chains longer than 2 hops — creates fragile distributed monolith","Extracting all services simultaneously — overwhelms the team's operational capacity","Skipping observability setup — debugging distributed systems without tracing is brutal","Building a service mesh before you need one — start simple, add complexity when pain is real","Neglecting the monolith during migration — it still runs in production, keep it healthy"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition — aligns cleanly with domain boundaries and stated constraints","Location Service isolation — clear technical and domain justification","Payment Service strict consistency — well-understood pattern","Migration strategy — Strangler Fig is the industry standard for this scenario"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput headroom for Location Service — 5K writes/sec is stated but growth trajectory unknown; if 10x growth is expected within a year, the architecture holds but technology choices may differ","Team's actual operational maturity — 'moderate' is subjective; if the team has never operated a message broker, Phase 1 timeline may be optimistic","Dispatch Service complexity — routing algorithm sophistication and real-time requirements could push this toward a more complex architecture (e.g., streaming pipeline) depending on business needs"],"assumptions":["8 engineers is the stable team size for the next 12+ months","The monolith is a single deployable unit with a shared relational database","The team has access to managed cloud services (managed DB, managed message broker)","5K writes/sec is peak, not sustained — sustained would require different write path design","No regulatory requirement to keep payment data in a specific geographic region (would affect service deployment topology)"]}}

Build a dead-letter queue for failed events with manual replay tooling. Start with simple timeout-based compensation."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Strangler Fig migration in 3 phases over 6-9 months. PHASE 1 (Months 1-3): Extract Location Service. It has the most distinct technical requirements (time-series writes at 5K/sec), minimal business logic coupling, and clear API boundaries. Stand up the event broker and observability stack during this phase. PHASE 2 (Months 3-6): Extract Payment Service. Strict consistency requirements make it critical to isolate early. This also decouples the most sensitive regulatory surface area. PHASE 3 (Months 6-9): Extract Dispatch, Notification, and Customer services. By now the team has operational muscle memory. Dispatch is the most complex extraction (depends on Location reads + Order events) so save it for when the team is experienced. Keep Order Management in the monolith longest — it touches the most other domains and benefits from lessons learned.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Decision record for the 6-service split","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — Why async events over synchronous calls","docs/adr/003-location-service-data-store.md — Time-series DB selection rationale","docs/service-contracts/location-service-api.yaml — OpenAPI spec for Location Service","docs/service-contracts/events-schema.json — JSON Schema registry for domain events (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned, etc.)","docs/runbooks/saga-failure-handling.md — Failure scenarios and compensating actions","infra/docker-compose.local.yml — Local dev environment with all services + broker","services/location-service/ — First extracted service (Phase 1)","services/payment-service/ — Second extraction (Phase 2)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Database-per-service with no shared databases. Correlation IDs on all events and API calls. Schema versioning with backward compatibility. Circuit breakers on all synchronous cross-service calls. Health checks and readiness probes on every service. Structured logging with service name, correlation ID, and timestamp.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Shared database between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Synchronous call chains longer than 2 hops — creates fragile distributed monolith","Extracting all services simultaneously — overwhelms the team's operational capacity","Skipping observability setup — debugging distributed systems without tracing is brutal","Building a service mesh before you need one — start simple, add complexity when pain is real","Neglecting the monolith during migration — it still runs in production, keep it healthy"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition — aligns cleanly with domain boundaries and stated constraints","Location Service isolation — clear technical and domain justification","Payment Service strict consistency — well-understood pattern","Migration strategy — Strangler Fig is the industry standard for this scenario"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput headroom for Location Service — 5K writes/sec is stated but growth trajectory unknown; if 10x growth is expected within a year, the architecture holds but technology choices may differ","Team's actual operational maturity — 'moderate' is subjective; if the team has never operated a message broker, Phase 1 timeline may be optimistic","Dispatch Service complexity — routing algorithm sophistication and real-time requirements could push this toward a more complex architecture (e.g., streaming pipeline) depending on business needs"],"assumptions":["8 engineers is the stable team size for the next 12+ months","The monolith is a single deployable unit with a shared relational database","The team has access to managed cloud services (managed DB, managed message broker)","5K writes/sec is peak, not sustained — sustained would require different write path design","No regulatory requirement to keep payment data in a specific geographic region (would affect service deployment topology)"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, aligned along domain seams. The decomposition prioritizes deployment independence (the stated goal), respects the high-throughput location tracking constraint (5K writes/sec), isolates payment consistency requirements, and stays within the operational capacity of an 8-engineer team. A Strangler Fig migration strategy is recommended — extract high-value services first (Location Tracking, then Payments), leaving lower-risk domains for later phases.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle management: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate and orchestrates the fulfillment workflow by emitting domain events.","boundaries":"Owns orders table/collection. Does NOT own courier assignment logic or payment state. Communicates with Payment and Dispatch via async events and synchronous queries where consistency demands it."},{"name":"Dispatch Service","responsibility":"Courier assignment, route optimization, and dispatch decisions. Matches available couriers to pending orders using location data and courier availability.","boundaries":"Owns courier-to-order assignment state and routing algorithms. Consumes location data from Location Service via internal API. Does not own courier profiles or order details."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS coordinates. Optimized for high write throughput (5K writes/sec peak). Exposes current and historical position queries.","boundaries":"Owns all location/GPS data. Append-only write path. No business logic beyond geo-indexing and TTL-based retention. Isolated data store (time-series or Redis) — not shared with other services."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment authorization, capture, refunds, and ledger reconciliation. Enforces strict transactional consistency on all monetary operations.","boundaries":"Owns payment records, transaction ledger, and integration with payment gateway(s). Synchronous API for payment operations. No eventual consistency — uses ACID transactions internally."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Delivers customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Consumes domain events and applies notification rules/templates.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates, delivery preferences, and send logs. Stateless regarding business domain — purely reactive to events. Owns delivery channel integrations (SendGrid, Twilio, APNs/FCM)."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account management: registration, profile, preferences, authentication tokens.","boundaries":"Owns customer profiles and auth data. Exposes a lightweight identity API consumed by all other services for auth/context. Relatively stable — low change frequency."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"Synchronous REST/gRPC: POST /payments/authorize, POST /payments/capture, POST /payments/refund. Order Service awaits payment confirmation before confirming order.","coupling_type":"synchronous — justified by consistency requirement"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Async event: OrderConfirmed → Dispatch Service subscribes and initiates courier assignment. Dispatch emits CourierAssigned back.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (event-driven)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"Synchronous query: GET /couriers/nearby?lat=X&lng=Y&radius=R. Used during assignment to find closest available couriers.","coupling_type":"synchronous read — low-latency requirement"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Dispatch Service","contract":"Async event: CourierLocationUpdated (high frequency, fan-out limited to Dispatch). Used for live ETA recalculation.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (event stream)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async events: OrderConfirmed, OrderDispatched, OrderDelivered → Notification Service maps to customer-facing messages.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Dispatch Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async event: CourierAssigned, CourierNearby → triggers push notifications.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"Synchronous query: GET /customers/{id} for order context. Lightweight, cacheable.","coupling_type":"synchronous read — cacheable"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Async event: PaymentFailed, RefundProcessed → triggers payment-related notifications.","coupling_type":"asynchronous (fire-and-forget)"}],"data_flow":"1) Customer places order → Order Service validates, calls Payment Service synchronously to authorize funds. 2) On success, Order Service emits OrderConfirmed event. 3) Dispatch Service consumes event, queries Location Service for nearby couriers, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. 4) Couriers push GPS every 10s → Location Service ingests at high throughput, stores in time-series DB, streams updates to Dispatch for live ETA. 5) Order status transitions (dispatched, delivered) emit events consumed by Notification Service, which fans out to email/SMS/push. 6) On delivery, Order Service calls Payment Service to capture funds. Refunds follow reverse flow.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Strangler Fig Migration","rationale":"Team has 3 years of monolith — big-bang rewrite is high risk. Extract one service at a time behind a facade, validate, then cut over. Reduces blast radius."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Architecture (Async Messaging)","rationale":"Decouples services temporally. Notification, Dispatch, and Order don't need to be available simultaneously. Enables independent deployment — the primary goal."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its data store. Eliminates shared-database coupling which is the #1 monolith trap in decomposition. Location Service gets a time-series store; Payment gets ACID-compliant RDBMS; others use PostgreSQL."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service has asymmetric read/write profiles: 5K writes/sec vs. bursty reads for dispatch queries. Separate write (append-only ingestion) and read (geo-indexed query) paths."},{"pattern":"API Gateway","rationale":"Single entry point for external clients. Handles auth, rate limiting, and routes to appropriate service. Prevents clients from coupling to internal service topology."},{"pattern":"Saga (Order Fulfillment)","rationale":"Order placement spans Payment and Dispatch. Use a choreography-based saga: Order Service emits events, each service reacts and emits completion/failure. Compensating actions handle failures (e.g., release payment auth if dispatch fails)."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: OrderId, contains OrderItems, tracks OrderStatus lifecycle)","OrderFulfillment (tracks assignment-to-delivery progress)"]},{"name":"Dispatch & Routing","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: AssignmentId, links OrderId to CourierId, tracks acceptance/rejection)","CourierAvailability (root: CourierId, tracks on-duty status, current capacity)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: CourierId, current lat/lng/timestamp, append-only history)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: TransactionId, links to OrderId, tracks auth/capture/refund lifecycle)","Ledger (append-only record of all monetary movements)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: NotificationId, channel, recipient, template, delivery status)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"generic","aggregates":["CustomerProfile (root: CustomerId, contact info, preferences, auth credentials)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderConfirmed; Dispatch consumes it. Order team accommodates Dispatch needs in event schema."},{"upstream":"Location Tracking","downstream":"Dispatch & Routing","relationship":"Conformist. Dispatch conforms to Location's position data model. Location optimizes for throughput, not Dispatch convenience."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Partnership. Both teams must coordinate on payment lifecycle events. Shared integration tests."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Open Host Service. Order Management publishes well-defined events; Notification Service maps them to templates. No upstream accommodation needed."},{"upstream":"Dispatch & Routing","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Open Host Service. Same pattern — Dispatch events trigger notifications."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (minimal). CustomerId type shared across contexts. Customer Service is upstream authority on identity."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services for 8 engineers (ratio ~1.3 engineers per service)","gains":"Clean domain boundaries, independent deployment (stated goal), each service is cognitively manageable","sacrifices":"Operational overhead — 8 engineers must operate 6 services + message broker + API gateway. On-call rotation is thin.","alternatives_considered":["Fewer services (3-4): merge Order+Dispatch+Customer into one. Lower ops overhead but reintroduces deployment coupling between order and dispatch — contradicts primary goal.","More services (8+): e.g., separate Courier Profile service. Over-decomposition for team size — would create excessive cross-service coordination overhead."]},{"decision":"Async events as primary integration (except Payment)","gains":"Temporal decoupling, independent deployability, natural resilience to partial outages","sacrifices":"Eventual consistency — order status may lag behind reality by seconds. Debugging distributed event flows is harder than tracing monolith calls.","alternatives_considered":["All synchronous: simpler mental model but creates a distributed monolith — defeats the purpose.","All async including payment: violates strict consistency requirement for monetary operations."]},{"decision":"Dedicated time-series store for Location Service","gains":"Handles 5K writes/sec without contending with OLTP workloads. Purpose-built indexing for geo-temporal queries.","sacrifices":"Another database technology to operate and maintain. Team needs to learn time-series DB ops.","alternatives_considered":["Redis with geo commands: simpler ops, but less durable for historical tracking.","PostgreSQL with PostGIS: familiar tech, but write throughput at 5K/sec requires careful tuning and may contend with other workloads."]},{"decision":"Choreography-based saga over orchestration","gains":"No single orchestrator to become a bottleneck or single point of failure. Each service owns its reaction logic.","sacrifices":"Harder to visualize end-to-end flow. Debugging failures requires correlating events across services.","alternatives_considered":["Orchestration saga (central coordinator): easier to reason about but creates coupling — the orchestrator must know about all participants."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"New channels (WhatsApp, in-app) are additive — add a new adapter in Notification Service without touching any other service."},{"area":"Routing algorithms","evolution_path":"Dispatch Service encapsulates all routing logic. Can swap from greedy nearest-courier to ML-optimized routing without affecting Order or Location services."},{"area":"Payment providers","evolution_path":"Payment Service abstracts gateway behind an internal interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or adding a second provider) is isolated to Payment Service."},{"area":"Location storage technology","evolution_path":"Location Service owns its data store. Can migrate from TimescaleDB to InfluxDB or Redis without ripple effects, since consumers use the service API, not the DB."},{"area":"Event broker","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (SQS/SNS, Cloud Pub/Sub, or managed Kafka). Can upgrade to dedicated Kafka cluster if throughput demands grow, since services interact via topic abstractions."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order-to-Payment synchronous dependency","reason":"Order confirmation requires payment authorization. This coupling is inherent to the domain — you cannot confirm an order without authorized payment. Designed to be rigid because correctness trumps flexibility here."},{"area":"Event schema contracts","reason":"Once services depend on event schemas (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned), changing schemas requires coordinated versioning. Use schema registry and backward-compatible evolution from day one."},{"area":"CustomerId as cross-context identifier","reason":"All services reference CustomerId. Changing the identity model (e.g., merging accounts) has system-wide impact. Invest in getting this right early."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational overload: 8 engineers operating 6 services + infrastructure (message broker, API gateway, multiple databases)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Phase the migration over 6-9 months. Start with Location Service extraction (highest write pressure, most isolated). Invest in shared observability (distributed tracing, centralized logging) before extracting the second service. Use managed services aggressively (managed DB, managed message broker)."},{"risk":"Distributed debugging complexity — event-driven flows are hard to trace","severity":"high","mitigation":"Mandate correlation IDs on every event and API call from day one. Implement distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) as a prerequisite before the first extraction. Build a simple saga status dashboard."},{"risk":"Data consistency gaps during migration — dual writes between monolith and new services","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strangler Fig with event-based sync. During migration, monolith publishes events that new service consumes (or vice versa). Run shadow-mode validation before cutting over."},{"risk":"Location Service throughput — 5K writes/sec is manageable today but growth could push to 50K+","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Design Location Service write path as stateless and horizontally scalable from day one. Use a partitioned time-series store. Add a write buffer (e.g., Kafka topic) in front of the DB to absorb bursts."},{"risk":"Team knowledge silos — each engineer becomes sole expert on 'their' service","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Rotate service ownership quarterly. Require cross-service code reviews. Document service contracts and runbooks as first-class artifacts."},{"risk":"Saga failure handling — compensating actions for payment+dispatch failures are complex","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Enumerate all failure scenarios explicitly before implementing. Build a dead-letter queue for failed events with manual replay tooling. Start with simple timeout-based compensation."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Strangler Fig migration in 3 phases over 6-9 months. PHASE 1 (Months 1-3): Extract Location Service. It has the most distinct technical requirements (time-series writes at 5K/sec), minimal business logic coupling, and clear API boundaries. Stand up the event broker and observability stack during this phase. PHASE 2 (Months 3-6): Extract Payment Service. Strict consistency requirements make it critical to isolate early. This also decouples the most sensitive regulatory surface area. PHASE 3 (Months 6-9): Extract Dispatch, Notification, and Customer services. By now the team has operational muscle memory. Dispatch is the most complex extraction (depends on Location reads + Order events) so save it for when the team is experienced. Keep Order Management in the monolith longest — it touches the most other domains and benefits from lessons learned.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — Decision record for the 6-service split","docs/adr/002-event-driven-integration.md — Why async events over synchronous calls","docs/adr/003-location-service-data-store.md — Time-series DB selection rationale","docs/service-contracts/location-service-api.yaml — OpenAPI spec for Location Service","docs/service-contracts/events-schema.json — JSON Schema registry for domain events (OrderConfirmed, CourierAssigned, etc.)","docs/runbooks/saga-failure-handling.md — Failure scenarios and compensating actions","infra/docker-compose.local.yml — Local dev environment with all services + broker","services/location-service/ — First extracted service (Phase 1)","services/payment-service/ — Second extraction (Phase 2)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Database-per-service with no shared databases. Correlation IDs on all events and API calls. Schema versioning with backward compatibility. Circuit breakers on all synchronous cross-service calls. Health checks and readiness probes on every service. Structured logging with service name, correlation ID, and timestamp.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Shared database between services — this recreates monolith coupling at the data layer","Synchronous call chains longer than 2 hops — creates fragile distributed monolith","Extracting all services simultaneously — overwhelms the team's operational capacity","Skipping observability setup — debugging distributed systems without tracing is brutal","Building a service mesh before you need one — start simple, add complexity when pain is real","Neglecting the monolith during migration — it still runs in production, keep it healthy"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary decomposition — aligns cleanly with domain boundaries and stated constraints","Location Service isolation — clear technical and domain justification","Payment Service strict consistency — well-understood pattern","Migration strategy — Strangler Fig is the industry standard for this scenario"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput headroom for Location Service — 5K writes/sec is stated but growth trajectory unknown; if 10x growth is expected within a year, the architecture holds but technology choices may differ","Team's actual operational maturity — 'moderate' is subjective; if the team has never operated a message broker, Phase 1 timeline may be optimistic","Dispatch Service complexity — routing algorithm sophistication and real-time requirements could push this toward a more complex architecture (e.g., streaming pipeline) depending on business needs"],"assumptions":["8 engineers is the stable team size for the next 12+ months","The monolith is a single deployable unit with a shared relational database","The team has access to managed cloud services (managed DB, managed message broker)","5K writes/sec is peak, not sustained — sustained would require different write path design","No regulatory requirement to keep payment data in a specific geographic region (would affect service deployment topology)"]}}
